    for (u, v, t) in tn.edges
        if !haskey(node_events, u)
            node_events[u] = Float64[]
        end
        if !haskey(node_events, v)
            node_events[v] = Float64[]
        end
        push!(node_events[u], t)
        push!(node_events[v], t)
    end
    
    burstiness_values = Float64[]
    for events in values(node_events)
        if length(events) > 1
            sort!(events)
            B = compute_burstiness(events)
            push!(burstiness_values, B)
        end
    end
    
    return burstiness_values
end

burstiness_vals = compute_node_temporal_stats(tn_activity)
println("Average node burstiness: $(round(mean(burstiness_vals), digits=3))")

7.2 7.2 Multilayer Networks

7.2.1 7.2.1 Mathematical Framework

Many real-world systems involve multiple types of relationships or operate across multiple organizational levels simultaneously. Multilayer networks provide a mathematical framework for representing and analyzing such systems.

Definition 7.10 (Multilayer Network). A multilayer network is a tuple M = (G, C) where G = {G^α | α ∈ L} is a set of layer networks G^α = (V^α, E^α) indexed by layer labels L, and C is a set of inter-layer edges connecting nodes across different layers.

The most common types of multilayer networks include:

  • Multiplex networks: Same nodes appear in each layer (V^α = V for all α), with different types of connections in each layer
  • Interconnected networks: Different node sets in each layer, connected through inter-layer edges
  • Interdependent networks: Layers depend on each other for functionality, creating cascading failure dynamics

7.2.2 7.2.2 Supra-Adjacency Matrix

The supra-adjacency matrix provides a unified representation of multilayer networks by concatenating all layers and inter-layer connections.

Definition 7.11 (Supra-Adjacency Matrix). For a multilayer network with L layers and n^α nodes in layer α, the supra-adjacency matrix A is a block matrix:

A = [A^11 A^12 … A^1L] [A^21 A^22 … A^2L] [ ⋮ ⋮ ⋱ ⋮ ] [A^L1 A^L2 … A^LL]

where A^αα are the intra-layer adjacency matrices and A^αβ (α ≠ β) are the inter-layer adjacency matrices.

using LinearAlgebra, SparseArrays

struct MultilayerNetwork
    layers::Vector{SimpleGraph}
    interlayer_edges::Vector{Tuple{Int, Int, Int, Int}}  # (node1, layer1, node2, layer2)
    layer_names::Vector{String}
end

function supra_adjacency_matrix(mn::MultilayerNetwork)
    """
    Construct the supra-adjacency matrix for a multilayer network.
    """
    n_layers = length(mn.layers)
    layer_sizes = [nv(layer) for layer in mn.layers]
    total_nodes = sum(layer_sizes)
    
    # Initialize sparse supra-adjacency matrix
    I_vals = Int[]
    J_vals = Int[]
    V_vals = Float64[]
    
    # Add intra-layer edges
    node_offset = 0
    for (α, layer) in enumerate(mn.layers)
        adj = adjacency_matrix(layer)
        rows, cols, vals = findnz(adj)
        
        append!(I_vals, rows .+ node_offset)
        append!(J_vals, cols .+ node_offset)
        append!(V_vals, vals)
        
        node_offset += nv(layer)
    end
    
    # Add inter-layer edges
    for (u, α, v, β) in mn.interlayer_edges
        # Calculate global node indices
        u_global = u + sum(layer_sizes[1:(α-1)])
        v_global = v + sum(layer_sizes[1:(β-1)])
        
        push!(I_vals, u_global)
        push!(J_vals, v_global)
        push!(V_vals, 1.0)
        
        # Add symmetric edge if undirected
        push!(I_vals, v_global)
        push!(J_vals, u_global)
        push!(V_vals, 1.0)
    end
    
    return sparse(I_vals, J_vals, V_vals, total_nodes, total_nodes)
end

# Create example multiplex network
function create_multiplex_network(n_nodes::Int, layer_names::Vector{String})
    """
    Create a multiplex network where same nodes exist in each layer
    with different connection patterns.
    """
    layers = SimpleGraph[]
    
    for (i, name) in enumerate(layer_names)
        g = SimpleGraph(n_nodes)
        
        # Different connection patterns for each layer
        if name == "friendship"
            # Small-world network for friendship
            p_rewire = 0.1
            k = 4  # Regular lattice parameter
            
            # Create ring lattice
            for node in 1:n_nodes
                for k_step in 1:(k÷2)
                    neighbor = mod1(node + k_step, n_nodes)
                    add_edge!(g, node, neighbor)
                end
            end
            
            # Rewire edges
            for edge in collect(edges(g))
                if rand() < p_rewire
                    rem_edge!(g, edge)
                    new_target = rand(1:n_nodes)
                    if new_target != src(edge) && !has_edge(g, src(edge), new_target)
                        add_edge!(g, src(edge), new_target)
                    end
                end
            end
            
        elseif name == "collaboration"
            # Preferential attachment for collaboration
            for node in 2:n_nodes
                m = min(3, node-1)  # Number of edges to add
                degrees = [degree(g, v) + 1 for v in 1:(node-1)]  # +1 to avoid zero degree
                probs = degrees ./ sum(degrees)
                
                targets = sample(1:(node-1), Weights(probs), m, replace=false)
                for target in targets
                    add_edge!(g, node, target)
                end
            end
            
        elseif name == "communication"
            # Random network for communication
            p_edge = 0.1
            for u in 1:n_nodes
                for v in (u+1):n_nodes
                    if rand() < p_edge
                        add_edge!(g, u, v)
                    end
                end
            end
        end
        
        push!(layers, g)
    end
    
    # Add inter-layer edges (same node in different layers)
    interlayer_edges = Tuple{Int, Int, Int, Int}[]
    for node in 1:n_nodes
        for α in 1:length(layers)
            for β in (α+1):length(layers)
                # Connect node to itself across layers with some probability
                if rand() < 0.8  # High probability for multiplex networks
                    push!(interlayer_edges, (node, α, node, β))
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    return MultilayerNetwork(layers, interlayer_edges, layer_names)
end

# Create and analyze multiplex network
mn = create_multiplex_network(50, ["friendship", "collaboration", "communication"])
supra_adj = supra_adjacency_matrix(mn)

println("Multilayer network created:")
for (i, name) in enumerate(mn.layer_names)
    println("Layer $i ($name): $(nv(mn.layers[i])) nodes, $(ne(mn.layers[i])) edges")
end
println("Inter-layer edges: $(length(mn.interlayer_edges))")
println("Supra-adjacency matrix size: $(size(supra_adj))")

7.2.3 7.2.3 Multilayer Network Centrality

Traditional centrality measures can be extended to multilayer networks, revealing nodes that are important across multiple relationship types.

Definition 7.12 (Multilayer Degree Centrality). The multilayer degree centrality of node v in layer α is:

k^α_v = ∑{u∈V^α} A^α{uv} + ∑{β≠α} ∑{u∈V^β} C^{αβ}_{vu}

where C^{αβ} is the inter-layer connectivity matrix between layers α and β.

function multilayer_degree_centrality(mn::MultilayerNetwork, node::Int, layer::Int)
    """
    Compute multilayer degree centrality for a specific node in a specific layer.
    """
    # Intra-layer degree
    intra_degree = degree(mn.layers[layer], node)
    
    # Inter-layer degree
    inter_degree = 0
    for (u, α, v, β) in mn.interlayer_edges
        if u == node && α == layer
            inter_degree += 1
        elseif v == node && β == layer
            inter_degree += 1
        end
    end
    
    return intra_degree + inter_degree
end

function multilayer_eigenvector_centrality(mn::MultilayerNetwork; max_iter=1000, tol=1e-8)
    """
    Compute eigenvector centrality for the entire multilayer network
    using the supra-adjacency matrix.
    """
    A = supra_adjacency_matrix(mn)
    n = size(A, 1)
    
    # Power iteration
    x = ones(n) / sqrt(n)
    for iter in 1:max_iter
        x_new = A * x
        
        # Normalize
        norm_x = norm(x_new)
        if norm_x > 0
            x_new = x_new / norm_x
        end
        
        # Check convergence
        if norm(x_new - x) < tol
            break
        end
        x = x_new
    end
    
    return x
end

# Analyze centrality in multilayer network
eigen_centrality = multilayer_eigenvector_centrality(mn)

# Map centralities back to layers
layer_sizes = [nv(layer) for layer in mn.layers]
centralities_by_layer = Dict{String, Vector{Float64}}()

node_offset = 0
for (α, name) in enumerate(mn.layer_names)
    layer_size = layer_sizes[α]
    centralities_by_layer[name] = eigen_centrality[(node_offset+1):(node_offset+layer_size)]
    node_offset += layer_size
end

# Find most central nodes in each layer
for (name, centralities) in centralities_by_layer
    top_indices = sortperm(centralities, rev=true)[1:5]
    println("Top 5 central nodes in $name layer:")
    for (rank, idx) in enumerate(top_indices)
        println("  $rank. Node $idx (centrality: $(round(centralities[idx], digits=4)))")
    end
end

7.2.4 7.2.4 Diffusion on Multilayer Networks

Diffusion processes on multilayer networks exhibit rich dynamics as information or diseases can spread within layers and jump between layers through inter-layer connections.

Definition 7.13 (Multilayer Diffusion). The state evolution for linear diffusion on a multilayer network follows:

dx/dt = -Lx

where L is the multilayer Laplacian matrix derived from the supra-adjacency matrix, and x is the state vector concatenating node states across all layers.

function multilayer_laplacian(mn::MultilayerNetwork)
    """
    Compute the multilayer Laplacian matrix.
    """
    A = supra_adjacency_matrix(mn)
    D = Diagonal(vec(sum(A, dims=2)))
    return D - A
end

function simulate_multilayer_diffusion(mn::MultilayerNetwork, 
                                     initial_state::Vector{Float64},
                                     diffusion_rate::Float64,
                                     t_span::Tuple{Float64, Float64};
                                     interlayer_coupling::Float64=1.0)
    """
    Simulate diffusion process on multilayer network using differential equations.
    """
    using DifferentialEquations
    
    L = multilayer_laplacian(mn)
    
    # Scale inter-layer connections
    layer_sizes = [nv(layer) for layer in mn.layers]
    total_nodes = sum(layer_sizes)
    
    # Modify Laplacian to include inter-layer coupling strength
    L_scaled = copy(L)
    
    node_offset = 0
    for α in 1:length(layer_sizes)
        layer_range = (node_offset+1):(node_offset+layer_sizes[α])
        
        # Scale inter-layer connections
        for β in 1:length(layer_sizes)
            if α != β
                other_offset = sum(layer_sizes[1:(β-1)])
                other_range = (other_offset+1):(other_offset+layer_sizes[β])
                L_scaled[layer_range, other_range] *= interlayer_coupling
            end
        end
        node_offset += layer_sizes[α]
    end
    
    # Define ODE
    function diffusion_ode!(dx, x, p, t)
        dx .= -diffusion_rate * L_scaled * x
    end
    
    # Solve ODE
    prob = ODEProblem(diffusion_ode!, initial_state, t_span)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=0.1)
    
    return sol
end

# Simulate diffusion starting from single node
layer_sizes = [nv(layer) for layer in mn.layers]
total_nodes = sum(layer_sizes)

# Initial condition: high concentration at node 1 in layer 1
x0 = zeros(total_nodes)
x0[1] = 1.0

# Run diffusion simulation
sol = simulate_multilayer_diffusion(mn, x0, 0.1, (0.0, 10.0))

# Analyze diffusion across layers
function extract_layer_states(sol, layer_sizes::Vector{Int})
    """Extract time series for each layer."""
    layer_states = Dict{Int, Matrix{Float64}}()
    
    node_offset = 0
    for (α, size) in enumerate(layer_sizes)
        layer_range = (node_offset+1):(node_offset+size)
        layer_states[α] = sol[layer_range, :]
        node_offset += size
    end
    
    return layer_states
end

layer_states = extract_layer_states(sol, layer_sizes)

# Plot diffusion evolution
using Plots
p = plot(title="Multilayer Diffusion", xlabel="Time", ylabel="Total concentration")

for (α, states) in layer_states
    layer_totals = vec(sum(states, dims=1))
    plot!(p, sol.t, layer_totals, label="Layer $α ($(mn.layer_names[α]))", lw=2)
end

display(p)

7.2.5 7.2.5 Layer Aggregation vs. Full Multilayer Analysis

A fundamental question in multilayer network analysis is when to aggregate layers into a single network versus maintaining the full multilayer structure.

**Theorem 7.2 (Information Loss in Layer Aggreg

8 Chapter 8: Network Inference and Reconstruction

The fundamental challenge in network science is often inverted: rather than analyzing a known network structure, we must infer the network from observational data. This inverse problem—network reconstruction—arises across disciplines, from inferring gene regulatory networks from expression data to reconstructing social networks from behavioral observations. This chapter develops the mathematical foundations and computational methods for network inference, covering correlation-based approaches, causal inference methods, Bayesian structure learning, and modern deep learning techniques.

8.1 8.1 The Network Inference Problem

Network inference seeks to reconstruct the structure of an unknown network G = (V, E) from indirect observations. Let X(t) ∈ ℝⁿ represent the state of n nodes at time t, where n = |V|. We observe a time series {X(t₁), X(t₂), …, X(tₜ)} but not the underlying adjacency matrix A. The goal is to estimate A from these observations.

Definition 8.1 (Network Inference Problem). Given observations X = {X(t)}ₜ₌₁ᵀ from a dynamical system on an unknown network, find an estimator Â = f(X) that approximates the true adjacency matrix A with minimal reconstruction error under some metric.

The inference problem is fundamentally ill-posed: many network structures can generate similar observational data. This necessitates careful consideration of identifiability conditions and regularization strategies.

8.1.1 Types of Network Inference

Network inference methods can be categorized by their assumptions about the data-generating process:

  1. Static inference: Assumes the network structure is time-invariant
  2. Dynamic inference: Allows edges to appear and disappear over time
  3. Causal inference: Seeks directed edges representing causal relationships
  4. Associative inference: Identifies undirected associations between nodes

Each category requires different mathematical frameworks and makes different identifiability assumptions.

8.2 8.2 Correlation-Based Network Inference

The simplest approach to network inference uses correlation between node time series as a proxy for network connectivity. While naive, correlation-based methods provide intuition for more sophisticated approaches.

8.2.1 Pearson Correlation Networks

Given time series data X_i(t) for node i, the sample Pearson correlation between nodes i and j is:

r_ij = (∑ₜ (X_i(t) - μᵢ)(X_j(t) - μⱼ)) / (√(∑ₜ (X_i(t) - μᵢ)²) √(∑ₜ (X_j(t) - μⱼ)²))

where μᵢ = (1/T) ∑ₜ X_i(t) is the sample mean.

The correlation matrix R = {r_ij} captures pairwise linear dependencies. However, correlation networks suffer from transitivity effects: if node i influences j and j influences k, then i and k will be correlated even without a direct connection.

8.2.2 Thresholding Methods

A common approach creates binary networks by thresholding the correlation matrix:

A_ij = 1 if |r_ij| > θ, 0 otherwise

The choice of threshold θ critically affects network properties. Too low, and spurious edges appear; too high, and true edges are missed.

using LinearAlgebra, Random, Distributions, Graphs, Statistics

function generate_network_data(A, T, noise_level=0.1)
    """Generate time series data from a network AR(1) model"""
    n = size(A, 1)
    X = zeros(n, T)
    X[:, 1] = randn(n)
    
    for t in 2:T
        X[:, t] = 0.5 * A * X[:, t-1] + noise_level * randn(n)
    end
    
    return X
end

function correlation_network(X, threshold)
    """Infer network from correlation matrix with thresholding"""
    R = cor(X')  # Correlation between rows (nodes)
    n = size(R, 1)
    A_inferred = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        if i != j && abs(R[i, j]) > threshold
            A_inferred[i, j] = 1
        end
    end
    
    return A_inferred, R
end

# Example: Small network inference
Random.seed!(123)
n = 10
# Create a sparse random network
A_true = zeros(n, n)
for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
    if i != j && rand() < 0.2
        A_true[i, j] = 0.8 * (2*rand() - 1)  # Random weights
    end
end

# Generate data
T = 500
X = generate_network_data(A_true, T)

# Infer network
A_inferred, R = correlation_network(X, 0.3)

# Compute reconstruction accuracy
true_edges = sum(A_true .!= 0)
inferred_edges = sum(A_inferred .!= 0)
correct_edges = sum((A_true .!= 0) .& (A_inferred .!= 0))

precision = correct_edges / inferred_edges
recall = correct_edges / true_edges
f1_score = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)

println("True edges: $true_edges")
println("Inferred edges: $inferred_edges")  
println("Precision: $(round(precision, digits=3))")
println("Recall: $(round(recall, digits=3))")
println("F1 score: $(round(f1_score, digits=3))")

8.2.3 Partial Correlation and GLASSO

Partial correlation removes indirect effects by computing correlations conditioned on all other variables. For multivariate normal data, the partial correlation between i and j is:

ρ_ij|rest = -Ω_ij / √(Ω_ii Ω_jj)

where Ω = Σ⁻¹ is the precision matrix (inverse covariance matrix).

Theorem 8.1 (Gaussianity and Conditional Independence). For multivariate normal variables, X_i ⊥ X_j | X_rest if and only if Ω_ij = 0.

This theorem motivates sparse precision matrix estimation for network inference.

8.2.4 Graphical LASSO (GLASSO)

The Graphical LASSO estimates sparse precision matrices by solving:

minimize_Ω  -log det(Ω) + tr(SΩ) + λ||Ω||₁

where S is the sample covariance matrix and ||·||₁ is the L1 norm promoting sparsity.

using Convex, SCS, GLMNet

function glasso_network(X, lambda)
    """Infer network using Graphical LASSO"""
    n, T = size(X)
    S = cov(X')  # Sample covariance matrix
    
    # Set up convex optimization problem
    Ω = Variable(n, n)
    problem = minimize(-logdet(Ω) + tr(S * Ω) + lambda * norm(Ω, 1))
    
    # Add constraint that Ω is positive definite
    problem.constraints += [Ω ≽ 0]
    
    solve!(problem, SCS.Optimizer(verbose=false))
    
    if problem.status == MOI.OPTIMAL
        Omega_hat = Ω.value
        # Threshold small values
        Omega_hat[abs.(Omega_hat) .< 1e-6] .= 0
        return Omega_hat
    else
        error("Optimization failed")
    end
end

# Apply GLASSO to example data
try
    Omega_hat = glasso_network(X, 0.1)
    A_glasso = (abs.(Omega_hat) .> 1e-4) .* 1.0
    # Remove diagonal
    for i in 1:n
        A_glasso[i, i] = 0
    end
    
    # Compute metrics
    correct_glasso = sum((A_true .!= 0) .& (A_glasso .!= 0))
    precision_glasso = correct_glasso / sum(A_glasso .!= 0)
    recall_glasso = correct_glasso / sum(A_true .!= 0)
    
    println("\nGLASSO Results:")
    println("Precision: $(round(precision_glasso, digits=3))")
    println("Recall: $(round(recall_glasso, digits=3))")
catch e
    println("GLASSO failed: $e")
    println("Using simpler partial correlation approach...")
end

8.3 8.3 Granger Causality for Network Reconstruction

Granger causality provides a framework for inferring directed networks from time series data by testing whether past values of one variable improve prediction of another.

Definition 8.2 (Granger Causality). Variable X Granger-causes variable Y if past values of X contain information that helps predict Y beyond what is contained in past values of Y alone.

Formally, X Granger-causes Y if:

Var(Y(t) | Y(t-1), ..., Y(t-p)) > Var(Y(t) | Y(t-1), ..., Y(t-p), X(t-1), ..., X(t-p))

for some lag p > 0.

8.3.1 Vector Autoregression (VAR) Models

Granger causality is typically assessed using Vector Autoregressive (VAR) models:

X(t) = ∑ᵏᵢ₌₁ A_i X(t-i) + ε(t)

where A_i are coefficient matrices and ε(t) is white noise.

Theorem 8.2 (Granger Causality in VAR). In a VAR(p) model, variable j Granger-causes variable i if any coefficient (A_k)_ij ≠ 0 for k = 1, …, p.

using GLM, DataFrames, StatsModels

function fit_var_model(X, p)
    """Fit VAR(p) model to multivariate time series"""
    n, T = size(X)
    
    # Prepare lagged data
    Y = X[:, (p+1):T]  # Response variables
    Z = zeros(n*p, T-p)  # Lagged predictors
    
    for k in 1:p
        Z[((k-1)*n+1):(k*n), :] = X[:, (p+1-k):(T-k)]
    end
    
    # Fit separate regression for each variable
    coefficients = zeros(n, n*p)
    residuals = zeros(n, T-p)
    
    for i in 1:n
        y = Y[i, :]
        X_reg = Z'
        
        # OLS regression
        β = (X_reg' * X_reg) \ (X_reg' * y)
        coefficients[i, :] = β
        residuals[i, :] = y - X_reg * β
    end
    
    return coefficients, residuals
end

function granger_causality_test(X, p, alpha=0.05)
    """Test pairwise Granger causality"""
    n = size(X, 1)
    T = size(X, 2)
    gc_matrix = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        if i != j
            # Fit unrestricted model
            coeffs_full, resid_full = fit_var_model(X[[i,j], :], p)
            rss_full = sum(resid_full[1, :].^2)
            
            # Fit restricted model (j doesn't cause i)
            X_restricted = X[i:i, :]  # Only variable i
            coeffs_rest, resid_rest = fit_var_model(X_restricted, p)
            rss_restricted = sum(resid_rest[1, :].^2)
            
            # F-test
            f_stat = ((rss_restricted - rss_full) / p) / (rss_full / (T - 2*p - 1))
            
            # Critical value for F-distribution
            # Using approximate threshold for demonstration
            if f_stat > 3.0  # Approximate F(p, T-2p-1) at α=0.05
                gc_matrix[i, j] = 1
            end
        end
    end
    
    return gc_matrix
end

# Apply Granger causality to generated data
gc_network = granger_causality_test(X, 2)

# Compare with true network
true_directed = (A_true .!= 0) .* 1.0
correct_gc = sum((true_directed .!= 0) .& (gc_network .!= 0))
precision_gc = correct_gc / max(1, sum(gc_network .!= 0))
recall_gc = correct_gc / sum(true_directed .!= 0)

println("\nGranger Causality Results:")
println("Precision: $(round(precision_gc, digits=3))")
println("Recall: $(round(recall_gc, digits=3))")

8.3.2 Multivariate Extensions

For networks with many nodes, pairwise Granger causality can be misleading due to indirect effects. Conditional Granger causality tests whether X Granger-causes Y conditional on all other variables Z:

X → Y | Z

This requires fitting full VAR models and becomes computationally expensive for large networks.

8.4 8.4 Transfer Entropy

Transfer entropy, introduced by Schreiber (2000), provides a model-free measure of directed information transfer between time series.

Definition 8.3 (Transfer Entropy). The transfer entropy from X to Y is:

TE_{X→Y} = ∑ p(y_{n+1}, y_n^{(k)}, x_n^{(l)}) log[p(y_{n+1}|y_n^{(k)}, x_n^{(l)}) / p(y_{n+1}|y_n^{(k)})]

where y_n^{(k)} represents k past values of Y and x_n^{(l)} represents l past values of X.

Transfer entropy measures the reduction in uncertainty about Y’s future when X’s past is known, beyond what Y’s own past provides.

8.4.1 Properties of Transfer Entropy

  1. Non-negativity: TE_{X→Y} ≥ 0
  2. Asymmetry: Generally TE_{X→Y} ≠ TE_{Y→X}
  3. Model-free: Does not assume specific functional relationships
  4. Reduces to Granger causality: For Gaussian variables in linear systems
using Distances, NearestNeighbors

function transfer_entropy(x, y, k=1, l=1)
    """Compute transfer entropy from x to y using k-nearest neighbors"""
    n = length(x) - max(k, l)
    
    # Construct embedding vectors
    Y_future = y[(max(k,l)+1):end]
    Y_past = embed_vector(y, k, max(k,l))
    X_past = embed_vector(x, l, max(k,l))
    XY_past = hcat(Y_past, X_past)
    
    te = 0.0
    for i in 1:n
        # Find k-nearest neighbors in joint space
        dists_joint = [euclidean(XY_past[i,:], XY_past[j,:]) for j in 1:n if j != i]
        eps = sort(dists_joint)[min(3, length(dists_joint))]  # Use 3rd nearest neighbor distance
        
        # Count neighbors in marginal spaces
        n_y = sum([euclidean(Y_past[i,:], Y_past[j,:]) < eps for j in 1:n if j != i])
        n_xy = sum([euclidean(XY_past[i,:], XY_past[j,:]) < eps for j in 1:n if j != i])
        n_y_future = sum([euclidean([Y_past[i,:]; Y_future[i]], [Y_past[j,:]; Y_future[j]]) < eps for j in 1:n if j != i])
        n_xy_future = sum([euclidean([XY_past[i,:]; Y_future[i]], [XY_past[j,:]; Y_future[j]]) < eps for j in 1:n if j != i])
        
        # Avoid log(0)
        if n_y > 0 && n_xy > 0 && n_y_future > 0 && n_xy_future > 0
            te += log(n_y * n_xy_future / (n_xy * n_y_future))
        end
    end
    
    return te / n
end

function embed_vector(x, dim, delay)
    """Create time-delay embedding of time series"""
    n = length(x) - (dim-1)*delay
    embedded = zeros(n, dim)
    
    for i in 1:n
        for j in 1:dim
            embedded[i, j] = x[i + (j-1)*delay]
        end
    end
    
    return embedded
end

# Compute transfer entropy network
function te_network(X, k=1, l=1, threshold=0.01)
    """Infer directed network using transfer entropy"""
    n = size(X, 1)
    TE_matrix = zeros(n, n)
    
    for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
        if i != j
            TE_matrix[i, j] = transfer_entropy(X[j, :], X[i, :], k, l)
        end
    end
    
    # Threshold to create binary network
    A_te = (TE_matrix .> threshold) .* 1.0
    return A_te, TE_matrix
end

# Apply transfer entropy (simplified version for demonstration)
# In practice, use specialized packages like TransferEntropy.jl
println("\nTransfer Entropy network inference would be applied here")
println("Requires specialized statistical estimation methods")

8.5 8.5 Bayesian Network Structure Learning

Bayesian networks represent probabilistic dependencies using directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). Structure learning infers both the network topology and conditional probability distributions from data.

Definition 8.4 (Bayesian Network). A Bayesian network is a pair (G, Θ) where G = (V, E) is a DAG and Θ specifies conditional probability distributions P(X_i | Pa(X_i)) for each node i, where Pa(X_i) are the parents of node i in G.

The joint probability factorizes as:

P(X_1, ..., X_n) = ∏ᵢ P(X_i | Pa(X_i))

8.5.1 Score-Based Learning

Score-based methods evaluate network structures using scoring functions that trade off model fit and complexity.

Definition 8.5 (Bayesian Information Criterion). For a Bayesian network structure G with parameters Θ:

BIC(G) = LL(G, Θ | D) - (k/2) log(N)

where LL is the log-likelihood, k is the number of parameters, and N is the sample size.

The challenge is searching the space of DAGs, which is super-exponential in the number of nodes.

using Combinatorics, Graphs

struct BayesianNetwork
    graph::SimpleDiGraph
    n_nodes::Int
    score::Float64
end

function bic_score(G, X)
    """Compute BIC score for a DAG structure given data"""
    n_nodes = nv(G)
    N = size(X, 2)
    
    log_likelihood = 0.0
    n_parameters = 0
    
    for i in 1:n_nodes
        parents = inneighbors(G, i)
        n_parent_configs = 2^length(parents)  # Binary variables
        
        # Count configurations (simplified for binary data)
        for config in 0:(n_parent_configs-1)
            n_config = sum(X[i, :])  # Simplified counting
            if n_config > 0
                log_likelihood += n_config * log(n_config / N)
            end
        end
        
        n_parameters += n_parent_configs
    end
    
    bic = log_likelihood - 0.5 * n_parameters * log(N)
    return bic
end

function greedy_structure_search(X, max_parents=2)
    """Greedy search for Bayesian network structure"""
    n = size(X, 1)
    
    # Start with empty graph
    best_graph = SimpleDiGraph(n)
    best_score = bic_score(best_graph, X)
    
    improved = true
    while improved
        improved = false
        best_local_graph = best_graph
        best_local_score = best_score
        
        # Try adding each possible edge
        for i in 1:n, j in 1:n
            if i != j && !has_edge(best_graph, i, j)
                # Check if adding edge creates cycle
                test_graph = copy(best_graph)
                add_edge!(test_graph, i, j)
                
                if is_cyclic(test_graph)
                    continue
                end
                
                # Check parent limit
                if length(inneighbors(test_graph, j)) > max_parents
                    continue
                end
                
                score = bic_score(test_graph, X)
                if score > best_local_score
                    best_local_graph = test_graph
                    best_local_score = score
                    improved = true
                end
            end
        end
        
        # Try removing each existing edge
        for edge in edges(best_graph)
            test_graph = copy(best_graph)
            rem_edge!(test_graph, edge)
            
            score = bic_score(test_graph, X)
            if score > best_local_score
                best_local_graph = test_graph
                best_local_score = score
                improved = true
            end
        end
        
        best_graph = best_local_graph
        best_score = best_local_score
    end
    
    return BayesianNetwork(best_graph, n, best_score)
end

# Generate binary data for demonstration
X_binary = (X .> 0) .* 1
bn_result = greedy_structure_search(X_binary, 3)

println("\nBayesian Network Structure Learning:")
println("Learned $(ne(bn_result.graph)) edges")
println("BIC score: $(round(bn_result.score, digits=2))")

8.5.2 Constraint-Based Learning

Constraint-based methods use conditional independence tests to constrain the set of possible structures.

Algorithm 8.1 (PC Algorithm). 1. Start with complete undirected graph 2. Remove edges between conditionally independent nodes 3. Orient edges using v-structures and propagation rules

The PC algorithm is sound and complete under perfect conditional independence tests, but real tests have finite sample limitations.

8.6 8.6 Network Inference from Epidemic Data

Epidemic data provides unique opportunities for network inference since infections follow network edges. The challenge is inferring who infected whom from temporal infection data.

8.6.1 Who-Infected-Whom Inference

Given infection times t_i for each individual i, we seek the transmission tree—the directed tree showing transmission pathways.

Definition 8.6 (Transmission Tree). A transmission tree T is a directed tree where edges represent transmission events, rooted at the index case.

The likelihood of a transmission tree depends on the epidemic model and network structure:

P(T | network, parameters) = ∏_{i→j ∈ T} p_ij(t_j - t_i)

where p_ij(τ) is the transmission probability between i and j with delay τ.

using Distributions, Random

struct EpidemicData
    infection_times::Vector{Float64}
    individuals::Vector{Int}
    n::Int
end

function generate_epidemic_data(A, β, γ, patient_zero=1)
    """Simulate SIR epidemic on network"""
    n = size(A, 1)
    
    # States: 0=S, 1=I, 2=R
    states = zeros(Int, n)
    infection_times = fill(Inf, n)
    recovery_times = fill(Inf, n)
    
    # Initialize patient zero
    states[patient_zero] = 1
    infection_times[patient_zero] = 0.0
    
    t = 0.0
    infected = [patient_zero]
    
    while !isempty(infected)
        # Compute next event time
        total_rate = 0.0
        
        # Transmission events
        for i in infected
            for j in 1:n
                if A[i,j] > 0 && states[j] == 0  # Susceptible neighbor
                    total_rate += β * A[i,j]
                end
            end
        end
        
        # Recovery events  
        for i in infected
            total_rate += γ
        end
        
        if total_rate == 0
            break
        end
        
        # Sample next event time
        dt = rand(Exponential(1/total_rate))
        t += dt
        
        # Determine event type
        r = rand() * total_rate
        cumulative_rate = 0.0
        
        # Check transmission events
        event_occurred = false
        for i in infected
            if event_occurred
                break
            end
            for j in 1:n
                if A[i,j] > 0 && states[j] == 0
                    cumulative_rate += β * A[i,j]
                    if r <= cumulative_rate
                        # Transmission i → j
                        states[j] = 1
                        infection_times[j] = t
                        push!(infected, j)
                        event_occurred = true
                        break
                    end
                end
            end
        end
        
        # Check recovery events
        if !event_occurred
            for i in infected
                cumulative_rate += γ
                if r <= cumulative_rate
                    # Recovery of i
                    states[i] = 2
                    recovery_times[i] = t
                    filter!(x -> x != i, infected)
                    break
                end
            end
        end
    end
    
    # Return only infected individuals
    infected_idx = findall(x -> x < Inf, infection_times)
    return EpidemicData(infection_times[infected_idx], infected_idx, length(infected_idx))
end

function infer_transmission_tree(epidemic_data, A_candidate, β, γ)
    """Infer most likely transmission tree given network structure"""
    times = epidemic_data.infection_times
    individuals = epidemic_data.individuals
    n = epidemic_data.n
    
    if n <= 1
        return SimpleDiGraph(n)
    end
    
    # Sort by infection time
    sorted_idx = sortperm(times)
    sorted_times = times[sorted_idx]
    sorted_individuals = individuals[sorted_idx]
    
    # Greedy assignment: each infected person caused by most likely predecessor
    tree = SimpleDiGraph(maximum(individuals))
    
    for k in 2:n  # Skip index case
        target = sorted_individuals[k]
        target_time = sorted_times[k]
        
        best_source = 0
        best_likelihood = 0.0
        
        # Consider all previously infected individuals
        for l in 1:(k-1)
            source = sorted_individuals[l]
            source_time = sorted_times[l]
            
            if A_candidate[source, target] > 0  # Must be connected
                delay = target_time - source_time
                if delay > 0
                    # Transmission probability (exponential waiting time)
                    likelihood = β * A_candidate[source, target] * exp(-γ * delay)
                    
                    if likelihood > best_likelihood
                        best_likelihood = likelihood
                        best_source = source
                    end
                end
            end
        end
        
        if best_source > 0
            add_edge!(tree, best_source, target)
        end
    end
    
    return tree
end

# Example: epidemic on small network
Random.seed!(456)
β, γ = 0.5, 0.2
epidemic_data = generate_epidemic_data(A_true, β, γ, 1)

println("\nEpidemic Inference:")
println("Total infected: $(epidemic_data.n)")
println("Infection times: $(round.(epidemic_data.infection_times, digits=2))")

if epidemic_data.n > 1
    inferred_tree = infer_transmission_tree(epidemic_data, A_true, β, γ)
    println("Inferred transmission tree edges: $(ne(inferred_tree))")
end

8.6.2 Network Structure from Multiple Outbreaks

Multiple epidemic outbreaks on the same network provide additional constraints for network inference. The key insight is that frequently co-infected pairs likely share network connections.

8.7 8.7 Inference from Diffusion Cascades

Information diffusion cascades reveal network structure through activation patterns. Unlike epidemics, diffusion cascades may have multiple activation mechanisms.

8.7.1 Independent Cascade Model

In the independent cascade model, each newly activated node has one chance to activate each inactive neighbor with probability p_ij.

Definition 8.7 (Diffusion Cascade). A cascade C = (S, T) consists of a seed set S and activation times T = {t_i} for each activated node i.

The likelihood of observing cascade C given network A is:

P(C | A) = ∏_{i activated} ∏_{j ∈ neighbors(i)} p_ij^{x_ij} (1-p_ij)^{1-x_ij}

where x_ij = 1 if i influenced j’s activation, 0 otherwise.

```julia function simulate_cascade(A, p, seed_nodes, max_time=10) “““Simulate independent cascade on network”“” n = size(A, 1) activated = falses(n) activation_times = fill(Inf, n)

# Initialize seeds
for s in seed_nodes
    activated[s] = true
    activation_times[s] = 0.0
end

newly_activated = copy(seed_nodes)
t = 1

while !isempty(newly_activated) && t <= max_time
    next_activated = Int[]
    
    for i in newly_activated
        for j in 1:n
            if A[i,j] > 0 && !activated[j] && rand() < p
                activated[j] = true
                activation_times[j] = t
                push!(next_activated, j)
            end
        end
    end
    
    newly_activated = next_activated
    t += 1
end

activated_nodes = findall(activated)
return (nodes=activated_nodes, times=activation_times[activated_nodes])

end

function cascade_likelihood(A, cascade_data, p) “““Compute likelihood of cascade given network”“” activated_nodes = cascade_data.nodes activation_times = cascade_data.times

if length(activated_nodes) <= 1
    return 1.0
end

likelihood = 1.0

# For each activated node (except seeds), determine possible influencers
sorted_idx = sortperm(activation_times)

for k in 2:length(activated_nodes)
    target = activated_nodes[sorted_idx[k]]
    target_time = activation_times[sorted_idx[k]]
    
    influenced = false
    
    # Check all nodes activated at previous time step
    for l in 1:(k-1)
        source = activated_nodes[sorted_idx[l]]
        source_time = activation_times[sorted_idx[l]]
        
        if source_time == target_time - 1 && A[source, target
